meta.title: Singapore's Infrastructure Readiness For EV
meta.subtitle: Will Singapore's power grid and public charging infrastructure support a 100% EV future by 2035?

items.secondary: IS602 - Spreadsheet Modelling for Decision Making

paragraphs.text: This study assesses whether Singapore's power grid and public charging infrastructure can support a complete transition to a full electric vehicle society by 2035. As of end-2024, electric cars account for approximately 7.5% of Singapore's total car population, up from under 2% just two years prior, with EVs representing about one-third of all new car registrations in 2024.
Hypothesis: Singapore's power grid and public charging infrastructure will be sufficient to support a complete transition to a full electric vehicle society by 2035.

The model uses BYD's Singapore model lineup (Atto 3, Seal, Dolphin, Sealion) as a representative sample to derive a defensible kWh/km electricity consumption figure, replacing generic industry assumptions with a brand-specific, publicly verifiable figure.

items.primary: Will Singapore's electricity grid maintain sufficient reserve margin to absorb peak EV charging load by 2035?
Is the projected national public charging infrastructure (60,000 charger target by 2030) adequate for the growing EV fleet?
Is public charging resources equitably distributed across Singapore's 55 URA planning areas?

tabsContent.title: Data Sources
tabsContent.description: Key datasets used to build and calibrate the model:

items.primary: LTA - Motor Vehicle Population by Type & Fuel Type
MOT EV Roadmap / LTA EV Targets

items.secondary: Battery capacity & range for Module 1 demand calc

sections.heading: Overall Black Box Model
sections.body: Variables are arranged into three layers: controllable inputs (government incentives, COE supply policy, charger deployment rate, TOU pricing, infra budget allocation), uncontrollable parameters (battery efficiency, EV price trends, average annual km, simultaneous charging rate, population density), and outputs (Grid Sufficiency Ratio, Grid Overload Risk Score, EV-to-Charger Ratio, Spatial Equity Score).

sections.heading: Four Interdependent Modules
sections.body: Module 1 (EV Adoption & Electricity Demand) is the sole point of dependency for all other modules - its outputs cascade into Module 2 (Peak Load Impact on Grid), Module 3 (Charging Infrastructure Sufficiency), and Module 4 (Spatial Distribution). Any change to adoption assumptions propagates through all four performance metrics.

sections.heading: Scenario Modelling
sections.body: Three scenarios - Conservative, Base, and Accelerated - are controlled via Excel Scenario Manager on Module 1 parameters, using a logistic (S-curve) adoption function, and propagate automatically to all downstream modules and the Dashboard.

tabsContent.title: Scenario Parameters
rows: Slower policy pull-through; incentives partially effective; adoption peaks late in decade
Aligned with government targets; sustained EEAI incentives; steady charger rollout
Strong policy push; high incentive uptake; early fleet saturation - maximum stress case

items.title: Total Car Fleet
items.caption: 659,889 vehicles (fixed) - EVs replace retiring ICE as COE expires; fleet does not grow

items.title: Annual km / kWh per Vehicle
items.caption: 17,000 km/yr × 0.2001 kWh/km = 3,402 kWh/yr (BYD WLTP 0.174 × 1.15 real-world uplift)

items.title: Peak Charging Window & Fraction
items.caption: 7:00-11:00 pm weekdays; 60% of fleet charging simultaneously

items.title: Charger Mix & Weighted Power
items.caption: 20% fast DC (50 kW) / 80% slow AC (7.4 kW) → 15.92 kW average

items.title: Study Scope
items.caption: Public charging only (HDB/URA carparks, petrol stations); private home/workplace charging excluded

content.text: By 2035 there will be 512,874 EVs (77.7% fleet share), annual demand of 1,744.6 GWh, with a peak load of 4,899 MW. The EV energy share of 1.95% appears low but does not indicate grid safety during peak demand.

EMA's 15% reserve margin will be breached in 2033, when it drops to 9.4%. By 2035, reserve margin will hit -6.2% and the Grid Overload Risk Score reaches 1.062 (≥1.000 indicates grid saturation), with peak demand of 16,746 MW against 15,761 MW capacity.

The EV-to-Charger Ratio (threshold of 10) is never breached across the full 2026 to 2035 window across all scenarios, reaching only 5.818 by 2035. Singapore's public charging infrastructure will be sufficient to support a complete EV transition by 2035, based on the LTA target of 60,000 chargers by 2030 and 8% annual growth thereafter.

16 of the 55 planning areas exceeded the ECR threshold of 10 by 2035, with 5 more on a WATCH list. The worst-performing area is Sengkang, with an ECR of 27.64, highlighting a structural gap between national-level sufficiency and local-level access.

Infrastructure subsidies have no meaningful impact on EV adoption, whereas an EEAI rebate of $10,000 per EV maximizes adoption at the lowest fiscal cost per EV, with $0 infrastructure subsidy per charger recommended as the most cost-effective policy lever.

Centralized Charging Hubs cover up to 1.45 EVs per $1,000 of government expenditure versus 0.32 EVs per $1,000 for fully Distributed Carpark Grants - a 353% improvement in cost efficiency - but centralization fails to achieve spatial equity, so the optimal mix depends on whether the government prioritizes cost efficiency or driver convenience.

items.title: Sensitivity Analysis: Grid is the Binding Constraint

content.text: 15 out of 25 modelled scenarios show a risk score above the 1.00 threshold. The grid risk profile is significantly more sensitive to capacity variations than to peak demand fluctuations, confirming infrastructure expansion as the primary binding constraint for long-term viability.

tabsContent.title: Conclusion Summary
rows: 16 of 55 areas breach ECR threshold; Sengkang ECR = 27.64
